coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of 17β-estradiol based on shaddock peel derived fluorescent aptasensor for forensic examination
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Zijia
Ling, Jiang
Zhang, Wenqi
Ding, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: 17β-estradiol (E2) detection technique had been shown to a potent method for identification of female blood in forensic practice since it was abundant in the healthy female body. Herein, we developed a fluorescent aptasensor based on carbon quatum dots (CQDs) derived from shaddock peel green synthesis for rapid detection of E2 as a useful auxiliary tool of forensic examination. The CQDs conjugated to the aptamer achieved fluorometric detection of E2 in blood and the blood of healthy female from 12 to 60 years old could be sensitive detected with the limit of detection of 0.025 ng/ml, and the analytical process could be completed within 10 min. The aptasensor was also used to assay E2 in forensic samples including blood and blood stain. In all instances, the results were positive when mixed samples involving female sample. This fluorescent aptasensor was proved to be a green, rapid and sensitive detection method of E2, and it exhibited great potential in discrimination of female samples in forensic practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 17β-estradiol
Carbon quantum dots
Forensic examination
Female identification
Shaddock peel
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs-APT probe
Characteristics of CQDs and CQDs-APT probe
Stability and selectivity of the aptasensor
Detection of E2 in spiked blood samples and forensic samples
Discrimination of female blood stain for forensic examination

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: National Natural Science Foundation of China , Grant/Award Number: 81772025 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CQDs, (B) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, (C) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs (red curve), CQDs-APT probe (black curve) and coupled with 0.1 ng/ml of E2 (blue curve), the change of fluorescence intensity was 351.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of different addition of ethanediamine, (B) Influence of aptamer concentration on the response of the developed aptasensor for 1 ng/ml E2, (C) Influence of synthesis time on the response of the CQDs, (D) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity changes of the CQDs-APT probe for 1 ng/ml E2.

(A) The fluorescence stability test of CQDs-APT probe during 12-week storage. (B) Changes of fluorescence intensities of CQDs-APT probe in the presence of different endocrine disrupting substances, where F1 and F0 represented to the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-APT probe in the presence and absence of the target.
(A) Fluorescence measurements of CQDs-APT probe to different concentrations (0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 ng/ml) of E2, (B) E2 detection standard curve.
Schematic illustration of CQDs-aptamer for discrimination of female blood in forensic examination.
Comparison with different reported methods for E2 detection.
Recoveries of E2 in spiked serum samples (n = 5).
Comparison of E2 concentration in female blood samples determined by developed aptasensor and official GC/MS.
Specificity study of female identification in blood stain. P: positive, N: negative (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of 17β-estradiol based on shaddock peel derived fluorescent aptasensor for forensic examination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 17β-estradiol (E2) detection technique had been shown to a potent method for identification of female blood in forensic practice since it was abundant in the healthy female body. Herein, we developed a fluorescent aptasensor based on carbon quatum dots (CQDs) derived from shaddock peel green synthesis for rapid detection of E2 as a useful auxiliary tool of forensic examination. The CQDs conjugated to the aptamer achieved fluorometric detection of E2 in blood and the blood of healthy female from 12 to 60 years old could be sensitive detected with the limit of detection of 0.025 ng/ml, and the analytical process could be completed within 10 min. The aptasensor was also used to assay E2 in forensic samples including blood and blood stain. In all instances, the results were positive when mixed samples involving female sample. This fluorescent aptasensor was proved to be a green, rapid and sensitive detection method of E2, and it exhibited great potential in discrimination of female samples in forensic practice.

ce:section.ce:para: Blood sample are one of the most crucial evidence in crime especially in violent crime, which can provide various biological information for criminal investigation and crime scene reconstruction. The conventional tests of blood focus on serological methods and DNA analysis, including real-time PCR [1], DNA methylation profiling [2] and the detection of genetic marker [3] such as short tandem repeat [4] and single nucleotide polymorphism [5]. Unfortunately, most of these methods are appropriate for single sample. However, the unknown blood obtained from crime scene is often dispersed or mixed, resulting in personal identification difficult. 17β-estradiol (E2), the most abundant female sex hormone in blood, is extremely important in the human body [6]. Although it can be found in male blood, the amount of E2 is well below par in a healthy woman [7,8]. Therefore, if the blood samples are from different sexes, we can distinguish the female blood from unknown samples, which is definitely beneficial to subsequent genetic identification. Traditionally, E2 can be detected by several instrumental analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography mass spectrometer (GC-MS) [9], which had the advantages of high accuracy and automatic detection. However, such methods are difficult and time-consuming, requiring expert support and sample preparation, which are far from satisfactory.

Recently, an effective and fast aptamer-based biosensor has been drawn more attention due to its simple synthesis, low labor-cost and high accuracy. Aptamers are single-stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides, which can bind target molecules [10] or proteins [11] with high sensitivity and specificity. Studies have shown that E2 can be detected through a stable DNA-E2 complex generated in a suitable condition [12], and the detection time can be within 50 min [13]. The aptasensor methods are powerful in the field of E2 detection, but its application is less in the discrimination of forensic samples. Besides, most methods reported previously are electrochemiluminescence with complex operation or fluorescence with suboptimal limit of detection, and the heavy use of organic chemicals tends to be environmentally harmful. Therefore, development of an environmental-friendly, simple and sensitive approach is still needed.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an excellent fluorescent nanoprobe, has generated a good deal of excitement in the areas of optical researches for past few years [14–16]. Compared to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots, its unique multifunctional properties make it more stable, less toxic and high functionalized. Recently, one step green synthesis of CQDs through hydrothermal method were reported [17]. In tradition, shaddock peel was the throwaway of shaddock, while the abundant carbohydrate makes them suitable for carbon precursor in synthesis of CQDs. Besides, shaddock peel contained various vitamins, mineral substance, organic acid and some naringin, which conferred many good properties on CQDs including water solubility, biocompatible capacity, and stable fluorescence. In this work, we designed a shaddock peel derived aptasensor and functionalized with ethanediamine, which has three-fold fluorescence intensity enhancement than the single fruit peel derived CQDs. The CQDs was conjugated with the DNA aptamer via the combination of carboxyl and amidogen, which was the recognition unit of E2. Under optimal conditions, this proposal aptasensor was successfully applied to the quantitative detection of E2 in blood and the examination of female blood sample.

1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA, https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/). 17β-estradiol and TE buffer (Tris-EDTA buffer containing 10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, PH=8.0) was purchased from Solarbio Science and Technology Company (Beijing, China). Estrone (E1), bisphenol A (BPA), tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBA) and ethanediamine were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Shaddock peel was obtained from red pulp honey pomelo which was purchased at the local supermarket. The known E2 aptamer [18] sequenced as CGCGCTGAAGCGCGG was synthesized and purified by Tsingke Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). The blood sample was collected from the Department of Forensic Science, Central South University, which was approved by the Ethics Review Board of Xiangya Medical College, Central South University. All reagents used in this study were analytical grade. All of the fluorescence measurements were carried out on a F-7000 Fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan) with 450 excitation. The transmission electron microscopic images (TEM) were measured by Titan G2 60-300 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). FT-IR spectroscopy images were obtained by FTIR-850 spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Sci.&Tech.Co,. Ltd, China) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs-APT probe
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared according to a hydrothermal method reported by earlier research [19] and we modified slightly. Pieces of shaddock peel were firstly cleaned and dried thoroughly at 40 ℃ overnight. Later, 0.2 g dry shaddock peel was mixed with 10 ml of ultrapure water and 0.5 mmol ethanediamine were added. Subsequently, the mixture was moved to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 600 min. After the product has cooled down, the solution was transferred to centrifuge tubes and centrifuged (4000 rpm, 30 min) to remove the impurity. Then, the supernatant was filtered by 0.22 µm microporous membrane and the CQDs was obtained.

Before adding the aptamer, the carboxyl groups of the obtained CQDs were activated by coupling agents. Briefly, 10 mg EDC and 10 mg NHS were added to the 1 ml CQDs solution, and the mixture was under stirred for 30 min. Then 100 μl DNA (10 µm) were added to the CQDs solutions and keep incubation at room temperature overnight to obtain the CQDs-APT probe.

In this work, the standard 17β-estradiol （E2） solution was diluted with ethyl alcohol and prepared in TE buffer. 40 μl probe solution was mixed with different concentration of E2 solution from 0.1 to 1 ng/ml. After the solution was incubated for 10 min at room temperature, the fluorescence was recorded when it was excited at 450 nm. In addition, other endocrine disrupting chemicals such as estrone (E1), bisphenol A (BPA) and tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBA) were added to the fluorescence sensor to explore the selectivity of the CQDs-APT probe to E2.

To evaluate the biological applicability of CQDs-APT probe in real sample and investigate the recovery tests of E2, two types of blood samples (spiked blood and forensic blood) were prepared: First, the blood samples from healthy male were centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 10 min and the supernatant was diluted 500 times with TE buffer. Then different concentrations of E2 were added to this solution and the spiked blood samples were obtained. The forensic blood samples were collected from the cases involving female that were admitted by the Department of Forensic Science, Central South University. After centrifugation and preparation, the solution was detected with the same procedure as detection in TE solution.

The blood stain was prepared according to the previous literature [20]. The male and female blood were respectively dropped in the several clean filter papers at first. Then the filter papers were dried overnight at room temperature and the blood stain was obtained. Before the fluorescence analysis, the blood stain was cut into pieces and dipped in ethyl alcohol for full extraction. After several times of centrifugation and dilution, the pretreated solution was mixed with probe solution for 10 min of incubation, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded. The blank filter paper without blood stain was used to do the control experiment.

The principle of fluorescent aptasensor for the rapid detection of E2 was illustrated in Scheme 1. CQDs were synthesized by a one-step green approach with shaddock peel as a carbon source and ethanediamine as nitrogen source. The fluorescence of CQDs is quenched when the aptamer was added, and turned on after the addition of E2, which can be attributed to the surface adsorbs of CQDs and the formation of CQDs-aptamer complex. It was reported that carbon nanoparticle doped with heteroatom has shown excellent physical and chemical properties [21–23], on account of its appropriate atomic size and five valence electrons which facilitated the combination with carbon atom [24]. Besides, the π-π interactions between the aptamer and CQDs could be induced easily because of the abundant functional group existed on the surface of CQDs, which leaded to the accumulation of the aptamer and CQDs. Under the activation of EDC/NHS coupling agents, the CQDs could adsorb the aptamer onto its surface through the interaction between carboxyl groups of CQDs and amino groups of DNA [25], and a complex of CQDs and aptamer was formed after adding the E2-aptamer [26]. Subsequently, the fluorescence signal of CQDs was decreased via a static quenching. Different to the dynamic quenching, static quenching occurred before the excitation and sometimes self-quenching could be happen since the distance between CQDs (wrapped with the aptamer) was shorter than before [27]. Upon the addition of E2, the specific aptamer wrapped around the target with strong affinity and released from the CQDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence. Consequently, the variation of fluorescence signal could be measured as a function of E2 concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of CQDs and CQDs-APT probe
ce:section.ce:para: The Fluorescence spectra, TEM images and FT-IR spectrum of the newly fabricated CQDs and CQDs-APT probe were explored ( Fig. 1). As shown in the Fig. 1(A), the nanoparticle of CQDs was equably dispersed in aqueous solution and displayed spherical shape with 3 nm diameter, which was in accord with the study reported previously [19].

The FT-IR spectrum exhibited the functional groups of the CQDs was shown in Fig. 1(B), and the absorption bands were 3408, 2964, 1641, 1568, 1402, 1120 and 1050 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at around 3408 cm−1 and the peak at 1402 cm−1 were ascribed to stretching vibration of N-H/O-H and C-N [28], which demonstrated the existence of amino group in CQDs. The band at 1568 cm−1 was attributed to CC stretching vibration, representing the existence of delocalized π-electrons [29]. The absorption peaks around 2964 cm−1 reflecting the C-H stretching vibration were observed. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 are corresponded to CO bond stretching [30], and the absorption peaks at 1641 cm−1 was detected for CO bond stretching [31,32]. The above result illustrated that a large number of functional groups existed on the surface of CQDs, stabilizing the nanoprobe in aqueous media.

As can be seen from Fig. 1(C), the fluorescence spectroscopy analysis showed that the emission peak of CQDs was 540 nm when excited at 450 nm (curve red). The fluorescence intensity was decreased with almost three-fold when the E2-aptamer was conjugated with CQDs (curve black) and it was recovered after adding 0.1 ng/ml of target E2 (curve blue). The above fluorescence results revealed that the shaddock peel derived fluorescent aptasensor was successfully developed on the quantitative detection of E2.

In order to obtain the optimum analytical performance, the influence of ethanediamine concentration, aptamer concentration, synthesis time and incubation time (between CQDs and aptamer) were optimized ( Fig. 2). As shown in the Fig. 2(A), compared to the pure shaddock peel derived CQDs without ethanediamine (curve black), the fluorescence intensity of CQDs with ethanediamine addition increased with two-fold at least and the emission peak was redshift from 450 nm to 540 nm. Also, we find the maximal fluorescence intensity when the addition of ethanediamine was 0.5 μmol. Besides, the effect of the concentration of aptamer was one of the most important parameters for sensitive detection of E2 (Fig. 2(B)). To explore the optimal aptamer concentration, we designed a series of concentration of the DNA and repeatedly added in the same volume of CQDs solution. When the addition of the DNA was 10−6 μmol, the aptasensor responded the strongest fluorescence intensity for 1 ng/ml E2. The possible reason was that the excessive DNA might increase steric hindrance and hinder the form of aptasensor [11].

Moreover, the synthesis time and incubation time between CQDs and aptamer were also optimized via time sequential experiments (Fig. 2). As presented in Fig. 2(C), the fluorescence intensity of CQDs raised with the increase of the heating time and started to descend when heated over 12 h during the synthesis procedure. Therefore, given the time-cost and energy saving, we chose the 10 h as the optimal synthesis time. In addition, the optimum incubation time of the present aptasensor were subsequently investigated. Fig. 2(D) displayed that when the same concentration of E2 was pipetted in the CQDs-APT probe, the fluorescence intensity raised rapidly in the first 10 min and remained at a stable level in 10–40 min, which is attributed to the combination of DNA and E2 reached the maximum and the formed complex could hinder the further combining process. The fluorescence intensity began to descend in 50 min might due to the decomposition of the E2. Given the saving reaction time and rapid detection of E2, 10 min was determined as the optimum incubation time for the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability was one of the most essential parameters for a fluorescence sensor, thus we investigated the stability of the aptasensor designed in this work. The CQDs was stored in refrigerator at 4 ℃ and kept in dark place for stability experiment. The fluorescence signal was recorded every four days, and it could be found in Fig. 3(A) that the intensity of CQDs remained 80% of its initial value after three months, which demonstrated the aptasensor has good florescence stability. The selectivity was another important property of a practical apatsensor. In order to verify the specificity of this fluorescence aptasensor, the effects of other endocrine disrupting substances including 1 μg/ml of bisphenol A (BPA), estrone (E1) and tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBA) were detected respectively, and the change of the fluorescence signal was shown in Fig. 3(B), F1-F0 represented the difference of the fluorescence signal in the presence and absence of the detection substance. From Fig. 3(B), we could observe that compared to the high intensity change for target E2, the relative fluorescence change of other endocrine disrupting substances (BPA, E1 and TBBA) was very little, which could be ignored to some extent. In combination with above results, we could deduce that this aptasensor exhibited good stability and high specificity toward E2.

In order to explore the sensitivity of this method, quantitative detection of E2 based on the CQDs-APT probe was carried out under the optimal experimental conditions ( Fig. 4). As depicted in Fig. 4(A), the fluorescence intensity exhibited a gradually increased tendency with the increase of the E2 concentration. From the result shown in Fig. 4(B), the good linear relationship could be found between the fluorescence change (F1-F0) of the CQDs-APT probe and the concentration of E2 from 0.025 ng/ml to 1 ng/ml, where F1-F0 represented the difference of the fluorescence signal in the presence and absence of E2. By calculating the value of the fluorescence change, the linear relationship was expressed as Y = 1039.3X + 167 with R2 = 0.9913 (Y is the change of fluorescence intensity, and X is concentration of E2) with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.025 ng/ml. It should be noted that the highly selective detection of E2 by the shaddock peel derived fluorescent aptasensor could be completed within 10 min. Compared to other selected previous methods for E2 detection in Table 1, although these methods had the advantages of fast response and easy operation, the developed aptasensor in our work achieved markedly improved detection sensitivity and dramatically shortened detection time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of E2 in spiked blood samples and forensic samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the aptasensor described here was further investigated to check whether the determination of E2 could be adapted to blood examination in forensic practice. Different concentrations of E2 were added in the serum samples after 500 times dilution and then the spiked blood samples were formed. The detection was under the optimum conditions within 10 min. The results were exhibited in Table 2. It was seen that the measured value was in accord with the spiked value, indicating the feasibility of E2 detection in serum sample.

To explore the possibility of this proposed aptasensor for forensic casework samples, we collected several female blood samples from the real forensic cases and firstly examined it using the aptasensor described here and official GC/MS method under the optimized conditions. The comparison results were listed in Table 3 and it was observed that absolute deviations were between – 0.033 and 0.052 ng/ml, further indicating its feasibility in forensic examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of female blood stain for forensic examination
ce:section.ce:para: In reality, most of blood evidence obtained from the crime scene existed in the form of stain, which was significant to genetic identification and break the case. Compared to the blood sample drew from the body, the blood stain was a trace of evidence and usually adhered to the surface of other materials. In this work, the blood stain was prepared in a filter paper to imitate the actual evidence (the blank filter paper without blood stain was proved to have no impact on the probe through the control experiment). The aptasensor efficiency about discrimination of female sample was tested through examining the single and mixed sample (containing female blood stain or not, Table 4). As shown in Table 4, the results of E2 detection were positive in all single and pooled female blood stain samples, which demonstrated that E2 could be a well indicator of female identification.

A fluorescence aptasensor was successfully developed for the rapid and sensitive determination of E2 for forensic examination. Compared to the traditional synthetic method, the synthesis of CQDs with shaddock peel as carbon sources was superior in simple operation, green synthesis and low cost. The CQDs-APT probe was formed as a fluorescence aptasensor to recognize and quantify E2 in the dynamic concentration range of 0.025 ng/ml to 1 ng/ml. Except for sensitive and selective detection of E2 in buffer and serum, this method exhibited a satisfactory discrimination ability of female sample, which was extremely important to distinguish the female blood from unknown samples in forensic practice, especially in sex crimes. Furthermore, the analytical process could be performed within 10 min, indicating the possibility of rapid on-site detection in the future. Above of all, compared to the traditional and the previous analytical methods, this study not only provided a novel way to green synthesis of CQDs and sensitive determination of E2 in a short time, but also was supposed to an effective tool to distinguish female samples for forensic examination.
The author declares that there is no conflict of interest that could be perceived as prejudicing the impartiality of the research reported.

Zijia Cheng: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology. Jiang Ling: Data curation, Methodology. Wenqi Zhang: Visualization, Validation. Yanjun Ding: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China, Grant/Award Number: 81772025.
